<!-- Topic 2: Addresses and Pointer Variables -->
<!-- Slides 14–27 -->

# Addresses and Pointer Variables
<!-- Slide 14 -->

## How Do We Store an Address? {.smaller}

- Obtain an address
- Store it in a pointer with the matching type

::: notes
Slides 14–27
:::

<!-- Slide 15 -->

---

## The Address Operator

```{.cpp}
int score = 88;

cout << score << "\n";
cout << &score << "\n";
```

- `&score` produces the address of `score`
- Address output normally appears in hexadecimal

<!-- Slide 16 -->

---

## Printing an Address

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    double temperature = 72.5;
    cout << "value:   " << temperature << "\n";
    cout << "address: " << &temperature << "\n";
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 17 -->

---

## Declaring a Pointer Variable

```{.cpp}
int score = 88;
int* scorePtr = &score;

cout << scorePtr << "\n";
```

- `int*` means pointer to `int`
- `scorePtr` stores an address as its value

<!-- Slide 18 -->

---

## Initializing a Pointer

```{.cpp}
int score = 88;
int* scorePtr = &score;
```

| Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `score` | the stored integer |
| `&score` | the address of the integer |
| `scorePtr` | a variable storing that address |

<!-- Slide 19 -->

---

## Reading a Pointer Declaration

```{.cpp}
int count = 4;
int* countPtr = &count;

double rate = 2.5;
double* ratePtr = &rate;

char grade = 'A';
char* gradePtr = &grade;
```

<!-- Slide 20 -->

---

## One Symbol, Two Jobs

```{.cpp}
int value = 42;

int& alias = value; // & declares a reference
int* ptr = &value;  // & obtains an address
```

- Declaration context creates a reference type
- Expression context asks for an object's address

<!-- Slide 21 -->

---

## Pointers Can Be Reseated

```{.cpp}
int first = 10;
int second = 20;
int* selected = &first;

cout << *selected << "\n"; // 10
selected = &second;
cout << *selected << "\n"; // 20
```

<!-- Slide 22 -->

---

## The Pointer Has Its Own Address

```{.cpp}
int score = 88;
int* ptr = &score;

cout << &score << "\n"; // score's address
cout << ptr << "\n";    // same stored address
cout << &ptr << "\n";   // pointer's address
```

<!-- Slide 23 -->

---

## Pointer Types Must Match

```{.cpp}
int count = 4;
double rate = 2.5;

int* countPtr = &count;
double* ratePtr = &rate;

// int* wrong = &rate; // compiler error
```

- The pointed-to type determines how the address may be used

<!-- Slide 24 -->

---

## Start With a Valid Address or `nullptr`

```{.cpp}
int score = 88;
int* ready = &score;
int* waiting = nullptr;

if (waiting == nullptr) {
    cout << "No score selected\n";
}
```

<!-- Slide 25 -->

---

## Putting the Syntax Together

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int first = 10;
    int second = 20;
    int* ptr = &first;

    cout << ptr << " " << *ptr << "\n";
    ptr = &second;
    cout << ptr << " " << *ptr << "\n";
    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 26 -->

---

## Pointers Store Typed Addresses

- `&object` obtains an address
- `Type* ptr` declares a pointer for that object type
- A pointer may store a valid address, be reseated, or deliberately hold `nullptr`

<!-- Slide 27 -->
